Q1: I tried to set up a consistent study routine, usually blocking out time in the mornings when I could listen to lecture recordings and podcasts related to the course material. Being an auditory learner, I found it easier to absorb information by hearing it rather than reading dense texts. I aimed for about 22 hours a week, spread across different days, but honestly my focus wasn't always there even when I was technically studying. I'd often have the materials playing while my mind wandered to other worries.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I'd like to admit. I started the semester with good intentions about regular study times, but as my anxiety built up and deadlines approached, everything became more chaotic. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course materials, then panic and try to cram everything in at once. The inconsistency made it harder to retain information, which only added to my stress.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I felt guilty about not keeping up, or when I saw notifications about new content or upcoming deadlines. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I was missing, which happened frequently given my terrible attendance record. The discussion boards also pulled me in because I actually enjoyed the peer interactions when I could manage my anxiety enough to participate.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine along with coffee. I'd scroll through new posts, watch a bit of video content, and try to engage with peers before my anxiety peaked for the day. But that habit broke down when things got overwhelming, and then logging in started feeling more like a source of stress than a helpful routine.
Q5: The recorded lectures and audio materials were genuinely helpful for me because I learn best by listening. I also found the discussion forums valuable when conversations got going—hearing different perspectives from my 46 or so peer interactions really helped concepts click. The video content was useful too, though I often wished it was longer or more in-depth since I'd only get through about 20 minutes before needing a break.
Q6: I consistently ignored or delayed the reading-heavy resources and text-based materials. My brain just doesn't process written information as efficiently, so I'd always prioritize audio and video content instead. I also tended to skip optional practice exercises, telling myself I'd come back to them later, but later rarely came. The irony is those were probably the things that would have helped my exam scores.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually feel myself understanding something new, especially when it connected to what we'd discussed in forums or what I'd heard in lectures. If I could listen to content and then apply it in a discussion with peers, that felt productive. Activities that were purely reading-based or felt disconnected from the bigger picture just drained my already limited motivation.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford mentally, even though I knew they might help. Required activities came with this weight of obligation and anxiety attached to them, which sometimes made me avoid them even more. It's strange—the optional stuff felt less threatening but also less urgent, so it always got pushed aside for things that had deadlines, even if I ended up not completing those either.
Q9: Absolutely, I downloaded a lot of audio files and lecture recordings to listen to offline, especially during commutes or when I was doing other things around my apartment. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos that covered similar topics because sometimes hearing a different voice explain something helped it stick. My notes from listening sessions were extensive, but none of that showed up in the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be lying in bed listening to downloaded lectures or reviewing my handwritten notes from what I'd heard, and the LMS would show nothing. I also spent time in study groups with other students where we'd discuss course concepts verbally—that's how I learn best—but there's no digital trace of those conversations. My most productive studying often happened away from my computer entirely.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download the audio versions of lectures or screen-record video content once, then replay them multiple times offline over the following days. The LMS probably showed one view, but I might have listened to the same material four or five times while doing chores or exercising. That repetition through listening was crucial for me, but it was completely invisible to the system.
Q12: Around midterms, my anxiety spiked significantly and everything became harder. I was dealing with medium-level stress that felt overwhelming combined with my high anxiety, and even though I have decent resilience, I hit a wall. My attendance dropped to almost nothing—that 10% figure is painfully accurate—and I started avoiding classes entirely because showing up felt impossible. The mindfulness intervention helped somewhat, but by then I'd already fallen behind.
Q13: My competing responsibilities and mental health absolutely affected my LMS usage. When anxiety was high, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without actually processing anything. Fatigue from poor sleep made it hard to concentrate even when I was technically engaged with materials. I'd often have multiple tabs open, technically logged into the LMS, but my brain was elsewhere dealing with worry and exhaustion.
Q14: Many times, unfortunately. I'd watch videos without really absorbing the content because my mind was racing with anxious thoughts. I'd scroll through discussion posts without truly reading them, just going through the motions. There were sessions where I was logged in for an hour but couldn't tell you a single thing I'd learned afterward. The engagement was superficial even when my activity metrics looked fine.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important—my 58% completion rate shows that clearly. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible; I'd tell myself I'd do it when I felt calmer, but that calm rarely came. Important tasks felt so high-stakes that avoiding them temporarily relieved my anxiety, even though it made everything worse long-term. It's a vicious cycle I couldn't seem to break.
Q16: Deadlines created panic rather than productive urgency for me. I'd ignore approaching due dates until they were almost upon me, then frantically try to complete work in a stressed state that didn't produce my best efforts. Sometimes I'd miss deadlines entirely because the anxiety of submitting imperfect work paralyzed me. The LMS deadline notifications actually increased my stress rather than helping me manage time better.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback temporarily boosted my motivation, but critical feedback—even constructive criticism—would send my anxiety spiraling and make me avoid that type of work afterward. I started checking for feedback less frequently because I dreaded the emotional impact. When my mood was already low, any feedback felt harder to process objectively.
Q18: The course structure with its many online components should have suited me given my resources and access, but the pacing felt relentless. Having 11 courses to manage meant I couldn't give this one the attention it needed. The structure assumed a level of consistent engagement that my mental health made difficult to maintain. I appreciated the flexibility of online materials but needed more scaffolding to actually use that flexibility well.
Q19: The availability of audio and video content made engagement easier for my learning style, and the active discussion forums gave me peer connection I valued. However, the heavy reliance on written assignments and text-based exams worked against how I learn best. The exam format particularly disadvantaged me—my 41% score doesn't reflect what I actually understood from listening to and discussing the material.
Q20: I think that pattern describes me somewhat—high engagement in terms of logging in and participating in discussions, but struggling to actually submit completed work. For me, it was anxiety about submitting imperfect work combined with difficulty translating what I'd learned auditorily into written assignments. You can be genuinely trying to learn and engage while still failing to produce the tangible outputs the system measures.
Q21: That's basically the opposite of my situation, but I can see how it happens. Some students might be efficient learners who don't need constant LMS interaction, or they might have strong external resources and study methods. For me, high LMS activity didn't translate to good performance because my engagement was often anxious and unfocused rather than productive. Activity and learning aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when and how often students access materials, which might indicate consistency or last-minute cramming. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether someone is actually learning or just going through motions while anxious. They also miss offline studying, peer discussions, and the mental and emotional state that determines whether logged activity translates to actual understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely connecting with the material in a way that changes how you think or what you understand. For me, that happens through listening and discussing, not necessarily through visible platform activity. True engagement involves curiosity and investment, not just compliance with logging in and clicking through content. My engagement was real in discussions and when listening to lectures, even if my outputs didn't reflect that.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show my extensive offline listening, my handwritten notes, or the genuine learning that happened in verbal study groups. It wouldn't capture how my anxiety affected the quality of my logged time, or how the mindfulness intervention helped me cope even if it didn't improve my grades. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that I actually cared about learning and tried hard—I just couldn't translate that effort into the metrics that mattered for passing.
